function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

results.content:
# FY2025 Segment Adjusted EBITDA by Segment

Based on the reconciliation table for the **Year Ended December 31, 2025**, the Segment Adjusted EBITDA for each segment is:

| Segment | FY2025 Segment Adjusted EBITDA |
|---|---|
| **Space** | **$653 million** |
| **Connectivity** | **$7,168 million** |
| **AI** | **$(1,237) million** |
| **Total Reportable Segments** | **$6,584 million** |

---

## Reconciliation Table (Year Ended December 31, 2025)

The table below reconciles **Income (loss) from operations** (the most directly comparable GAAP measure) to **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**:

| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Add: Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Add: Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

---

## How Segment Adjusted EBITDA Is Calculated

Per the filing, **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** is defined as segment income (loss) from operations excluding:

1. **Depreciation and amortization**
2. **Share-based compensation**
3. **Restructuring charges**
4. **Impairment**

### Worked Example — Space Segment (FY2025):
- Income (loss) from operations: $(657)M
- + Depreciation and amortization: $757M
- + Share-based compensation: $515M
- + Restructuring charges: $0M
- + Impairment: $38M
- = **Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $653M**

### Worked Example — Connectivity Segment (FY2025):
- Income from operations: $4,423M
- + Depreciation and amortization: $2,376M
- + Share-based compensation: $369M
- + Restructuring charges: $0M
- + Impairment: $0M
- = **Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168M**

### Worked Example — AI Segment (FY2025):
- Loss from operations: $(6,355)M
- + Depreciation and amortization: $3,568M
- + Share-based compensation: $1,063M
- + Restructuring charges: $487M
- + Impairment: $0M
- = **Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $(1,237)M**

---

## Key Contextual Notes

- **Space** Segment Adjusted EBITDA decreased by $501M year-over-year (from $1,154M in 2024 to $653M in 2025), primarily driven by accelerated investment in the Starship vehicle and launch facilities, partially offset by increased NASA CRS missions and a U.S. Department of War contract.
- **Connectivity** Segment Adjusted EBITDA grew significantly (86.2% year-over-year), driven by subscriber growth, enterprise adoption, and network efficiency improvements via the Starlink constellation.
- **AI** Segment Adjusted EBITDA decreased by $1,584M (from $347M in 2024 to $(1,237)M in 2025), reflecting the segment's earlier stage of development and heavy investments in cloud computing, data center infrastructure, and employee expenses. The company expects a multi-year investment horizon before sustained positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

# Segment Definitions and Descriptions

## Space Segment

**Definition:** The Space segment designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets to provide high cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at unprecedented scale.

**Products and Services:**
- **Falcon launch vehicles** (Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy), composed of boosters (first stages), second stages, Merlin engines, and fairings—boosters, fairings, and Merlin engines are reusable
- **Dragon spacecraft** for transporting astronauts, satellites, and other payloads
- **Starship** (in development)—a fully reusable transportation system designed to carry crew, cargo, satellites, and data centers to Earth orbit, the Moon, Mars, and beyond

**Revenue Categories:**
- **Launch Services** (recognized at point in time)—customer launches
- **Launch & Development** (recognized over time)—long-term government contracts

**Key Note:** Space segment revenue only reflects customer launches and customer activities. Launches dedicated to deploying Starlink satellites are capitalized within the Connectivity segment, and the company expects to allocate significant launch capacity to the AI segment in the future. As a result, the Space segment has relatively lower revenue scale and growth compared to other segments, though it provides foundational strategic value.

---

## Connectivity Segment

**Definition:** The Connectivity segment operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband data and communications network powered by over 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets.

**Products and Services:**
- **Starlink Consumer Broadband**—the world's largest and most advanced space-based internet broadband service, with median latency at approximately 25 milliseconds and median download speeds of 225 Mbps during peak hours for residential users (as of March 31, 2026)
- **Starlink Mobile**—satellite-to-mobile texting and over-the-top voice services, serving approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries
- **Enterprise & Government connectivity solutions**

**Subscriber Metrics (as of March 31, 2026):**
- Approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers
- Approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in orbit

**Revenue Categories:**
- **Consumer**
- **Enterprise & Government**

All products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment.

---

## AI Segment

**Definition:** The AI segment operates a highly vertically integrated AI platform spanning a frontier LLM, AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers, a real-time information platform, and AI computational infrastructure.

**Products and Services:**
- **Grok**—a truth-seeking frontier large language model
- **AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers**
- **X**—a real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform
- **AI computational infrastructure**, including terrestrial power and AI compute infrastructure, with plans for orbital AI compute satellite deployments

**Revenue Categories:**
- **Advertising** (primarily from X)
- **AI Solutions & Infrastructure**

**Background:** xAI was founded in 2023 and acquired by SpaceX in early 2026, becoming an integral pillar of the company's vertically integrated innovation engine. The segment is in an earlier stage of development, with the company planning to prioritize growth and investment to capture significant opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure.

---

## Segment Reporting Framework

Effective Q1 2026, following the Mergers, the company's Chief Executive Officer (as Chief Operating Decision Maker) organizes the company, manages resource allocations, and measures performance among these three operating and reportable segments. The CODM assesses performance and allocates resources based on **segment income (loss) from operations**, comparing actual results to historical results and previously forecasted financial information. Prior period presentations conform to the current three-segment reporting structure.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the FY2025 Segment Adjusted EBITDA figures:
- Space: $653 million
- Connectivity: $7,168 million
- AI: $(1,237) million
- Total: $6,584 million

Now I need to construct a sum-of-parts valuation framework using reasonable public-market comparable categories. Let me think about what comparable companies would be appropriate for each segment:

**Space Segment** ($653M Segment Adjusted EBITDA):
- This is a launch services and space manufacturing business
- Public comparables could include:
  - Rocket Lab (RKLB) - launch services
  - AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) - though this is more connectivity
  - Virgin Galactic (SPCE) - though this is space tourism
  - ILC Dover, Sierra Space (private)
  - Defense primes like Lockheed Martin, Boeing, Northrop Grumman (space divisions)
  - The challenge: SpaceX's Space segment is unique because it's the dominant launch provider with reusable rockets, but its revenue is constrained because Starlink launches are capitalized in Connectivity

**Connectivity Segment** ($7,168M Segment Adjusted EBITDA):
- This is a satellite broadband/communications business
- Public comparables could include:
  - Hughes Network Systems (EchoStar - SATS)
  - Viasat (VSAT)
  - Iridium (IRDM)
  - Globalstar (GSAT)
  - AST SpaceMobile (ASTS)
  - Telecom companies more broadly (Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile)
  - The challenge: Starlink is uniquely scaled with 9,600+ satellites and 10.3M subscribers, making it more like a global telecom than a traditional satellite operator

**AI Segment** (-$1,237M Segment Adjusted EBITDA):
- This combines:
  - Grok (frontier LLM) - comparable to OpenAI (private), Anthropic (private), Google Gemini, Microsoft Copilot
  - X (social media platform) - comparable to Meta, Snap, Reddit
  - AI compute infrastructure - comparable to CoreWeave (private), hyperscalers
  - The challenge: This is a hybrid business combining LLM development, social media, and compute infrastructure - no single public company captures all of these

Let me think about which segment creates the greatest comparable-selection ambiguity:

The AI segment likely creates the greatest ambiguity because:
1. It combines three distinct businesses (LLM, social media, compute infrastructure)
2. The most direct LLM comparables (OpenAI, Anthropic) are private
3. The social media comparable (X/Twitter) was taken private and is now embedded in this segment
4. The compute infrastructure comparable (CoreWeave) recently went public but is at a different scale
5. The segment is loss-making, making valuation multiples less meaningful
6. The segment includes plans for orbital AI compute, which has no public comparable

Actually, let me think more carefully. The Space segment also has significant ambiguity:
- SpaceX's Space segment is unique because it's the dominant launch provider
- Most direct comparables (Rocket Lab) are at much smaller scale
- The segment's revenue is artificially constrained because Starlink launches are capitalized in Connectivity
- Defense primes have space divisions but they're not pure-play
- The Starship development is a unique asset with no public comparable

But I think the AI segment has the greatest ambiguity because:
1. It's a hybrid of three very different businesses
2. The most relevant comparables are private
3. It's loss-making, so traditional valuation multiples don't apply
4. The strategic value of vertical integration (LLM + social data + compute) is hard to value
5. Future plans (orbital AI compute) have no precedent

Let me search for some peer company financials to support my analysis. Let me look at recent filings for:
- Rocket Lab (RKLB) - for Space
- Iridium (IRDM), Viasat (VSAT), EchoStar (SATS) - for Connectivity
- For AI, I'll need to think about Meta, Reddit, and possibly CoreWeave

Actually, let me first verify the SpaceX S-1 data and then look at peer companies. Let me also check if there's more context on the segments.

# SpaceX Sum-of-Parts Valuation Framework Using FY2025 Segment Adjusted EBITDA

## 1. FY2025 Segment Financials (from SpaceX S-1, retrospectively recast to include xAI and X Holdings)

| Segment | Revenue ($M) | Segment Adjusted EBITDA ($M) | EBITDA Margin |
|---------|-------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| **Space** | 4,086 | **653** | 16.0% |
| **Connectivity** | 11,387 | **7,168** | 63.0% |
| **AI** | 3,201 | **(1,237)** | (38.6)% |
| — Advertising (X) | 1,844 | — | — |
| — AI Solutions & Infrastructure | 1,357 | — | — |
| **Total** | 18,674 | **6,584** | 35.3% |

SpaceX balance sheet (Dec 31, 2025): Total debt $22,896M; Total cash $24,747M; **Net cash ≈ $1,851M**.

Segment Adjusted EBITDA = segment income (loss) from operations + D&A + share-based compensation + restructuring + impairment.

---

## 2. Public-Market Comparable Categories and Peer Multiples

Stock prices and share counts are as of late February 2026 (most recent available pricing data; SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026).

### A. Space Segment — Comparable Category: Space Launch & Defense

| Peer | Revenue ($M) | Op. Income ($M) | Market Cap ($B) | EV ($B) | EV/Revenue | EV/EBITDA |
|------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|---------|------------|-----------|
| **Rocket Lab (RKLB)** | 601.8 | (228.8) | 39.2 | ~38.9 | ~64.6x | N/A (loss-making) |

**Observation:** Rocket Lab is the only pure-play public launch-services comparable, but it is loss-making and ~7x smaller by revenue than SpaceX's Space segment. Defense primes (Lockheed, Northrop, Boeing) have space divisions but are diversified and trade at ~15–20x EBITDA. SpaceX's Space segment revenue is also understated because Starlink-deployment launches are capitalized within the Connectivity segment, and Starship (in development) has no public comparable.

### B. Connectivity Segment — Comparable Category: Satellite Communications Operators

| Peer | Revenue ($M) | EBITDA ($M) | Market Cap ($B) | EV ($B) | EV/Revenue | EV/EBITDA |
|------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|---------|------------|-----------|
| **Iridium (IRDM)** | 871.7 | ~480 | 2.51 | 4.18 | 4.8x | **8.7x** |
| Globalstar (GSAT) | 273.0 | n/a | — | — | — | — |
| Viasat (VSAT) | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| Gogo (GOGO) | — | — | — | — | — | — |

**Observation:** Iridium is the closest scaled comparable (satellite operator with positive EBITDA). Traditional satellite operators trade at ~6–12x EBITDA. However, Starlink's scale (10.3M subscribers, $11.4B revenue, 63% EBITDA margin) is an order of magnitude larger than Iridium and growing far faster, justifying a premium multiple.

### C. AI Segment — Comparable Categories: Multiple (AI Compute Infrastructure + Social Media/Advertising + LLM)

| Peer | Revenue ($M) | Adj. EBITDA ($M) | Market Cap ($B) | EV ($B) | EV/Revenue | EV/Adj. EBITDA |
|------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|---------|------------|----------------|
| **CoreWeave (CRWV)** — AI compute infra | 1,900 | 1,219 | 37.7 | ~45.7 | **23.8x** | 37.5x |
| **Vertiv (VRT)** — AI power/cooling infra | 10,230 | ~2,330 | 97.5 | 98.6 | **9.6x** | 42.3x |
| **Reddit (RDDT)** — social/advertising | 2,203 | 845 | 27.9 | 25.4 | **11.5x** | 30.0x |
| **Snap (SNAP)** — social/advertising | 5,931 | 689 | 7.7 | 8.3 | **1.4x** | 12.0x |

**Observation:** The AI segment's revenue splits between advertising (X platform, $1,844M) and AI solutions & infrastructure ($1,357M, including Grok subscriptions, data licensing, API, and compute). These two sub-businesses map to entirely different comparable sets with wildly divergent multiples. There is **no public pure-play LLM comparable** (OpenAI and Anthropic are private).

---

## 3. Sum-of-Parts Valuation Framework

| Segment | Metric Used | Multiple Range | Implied EV Range ($B) | Midpoint ($B) |
|---------|------------|----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Space** | EV/EBITDA ($653M) | 15–25x | 9.8 – 16.3 | ~13.0 |
| **Connectivity** | EV/EBITDA ($7,168M) | 12–18x | 86.0 – 129.0 | ~107.5 |
| **AI** | EV/Revenue ($3,201M) | 8–14x | 25.6 – 44.8 | ~35.2 |
| **Total Enterprise Value** | | | **121.4 – 190.1** | **~155.7** |
| Plus: Net cash | | | +1.9 | +1.9 |
| **Implied Equity Value** | | | **123.3 – 192.0** | **~157.6** |

**Rationale for multiples:**
- **Space (15–25x EBITDA):** Rocket Lab is loss-making so no direct EV/EBITDA benchmark exists; range blends defense-prime multiples (~15–20x) with a premium for SpaceX's dominant launch market share, Starship option value, and understated revenue (Starlink launches capitalized elsewhere).
- **Connectivity (12–18x EBITDA):** Premium to Iridium's ~8.7x reflecting Starlink's 10x larger subscriber base, 63% EBITDA margin, and rapid growth; capped below hyperscaler-type multiples given satellite capex intensity.
- **AI (8–14x Revenue):** Blended across the advertising sub-segment (comparable to Reddit at 11.5x / Snap at 1.4x) and the AI solutions & infrastructure sub-segment (comparable to CoreWeave at 23.8x / Vertiv at 9.6x). EV/EBITDA is unusable because the segment is loss-making at $(1,237)M.

---

## 4. Segment with Greatest Comparable-Selection Ambiguity: **AI**

The **AI segment** creates by far the greatest comparable-selection ambiguity, for the following reasons:

### (a) Hybrid business model spanning three distinct comparable categories
The AI segment bundles three businesses with fundamentally different unit economics into one reporting segment:
1. **Social media / advertising (X)** — $1,844M revenue → maps to Reddit/Snap comparables
2. **Frontier LLM (Grok)** — no public pure-play comparable (OpenAI and Anthropic are private; their valuations derive from private funding rounds, not disciplined public-market trading)
3. **AI compute infrastructure** — $1,357M revenue → maps to CoreWeave/Vertiv comparables

No single public company or comparable category captures all three. An analyst must either (i) split the segment and apply different multiples to each sub-business (introducing allocation subjectivity), or (ii) apply a single blended multiple that averages across categories with very different risk/return profiles.

### (b) Loss-making, forcing reliance on EV/Revenue with an enormous multiple spread
Because Segment Adjusted EBITDA is **negative $(1,237)M**, EV/EBITDA is mathematically meaningless. The analyst is forced to use EV/Revenue, but the relevant comparable revenue multiples span a **17x range**:
- Snap: 1.4x revenue
- Vertiv: 9.6x revenue
- Reddit: 11.5x revenue
- CoreWeave: 23.8x revenue

Selecting any point in this range is highly subjective and swings the implied AI enterprise value from ~$4.5B (at 1.4x) to ~$76B (at 23.8x).

### (c) No public LLM pure-play
Grok is the segment's flagship product, but the most directly relevant comparables — OpenAI and Anthropic — are private. Their reported valuations reflect late-stage private rounds that may embed liquidity discounts, strategic premiums, or momentum pricing not subject to public-market discipline. There is no way to triangulate a "market" LLM multiple from public filings.

### (d) Unprecedented strategic optionality
The S-1 discloses plans to deploy **orbital AI compute satellites as early as 2028**, with a long-term goal of launching **100 gigawatts of compute to space annually**. No public company has ever attempted space-based AI compute, so there is no precedent for valuing this optionality. Traditional comparables (CoreWeave, Vertiv) are terrestrial and cannot price the orbital compute thesis.

### (e) Vertical-integration premium is unquantifiable via standalone peers
The segment's strategic logic rests on combining Grok (LLM) + X (real-time data) + compute infrastructure + Starlink connectivity + orbital compute. This vertical integration creates synergies and data advantages that no standalone comparable possesses, making it impossible to derive an appropriate conglomerate premium from public peers.

### (f) Early-stage with multi-year investment horizon
The S-1 explicitly states the company expects a **"multi-year investment horizon before sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA,"** with Segment Adjusted EBITDA deteriorating from +$347M in 2024 to $(1,237)M in 2025. This negative trajectory and uncertain path to profitability makes any DCF or multiple-based approach highly sensitive to assumptions about future capex and revenue ramp.

### Contrast with Space and Connectivity
- **Space** has one clear pure-play public comparable (Rocket Lab); the ambiguity is mainly about scale mismatch and the Starship option value, not about which comparable *category* to use.
- **Connectivity** has a well-defined comparable category (satellite communications operators: Iridium, Globalstar, Viasat, Gogo) with established EV/EBITDA benchmarks; the ambiguity is about the magnitude of Starlink's growth premium, not about category selection.

Only the **AI segment** forces the analyst to choose among multiple, mutually inconsistent comparable categories, with no public benchmark for the segment's most important product (Grok), while the segment is loss-making and includes unprecedented strategic optionality (orbital compute). This makes it the segment with the greatest comparable-selection ambiguity.

---
